elements.sent-2411-001: The Burrows-Wheeler transform (BWT) is a reversible transform that converts a string $w$ into another string $\bwt(w)$.
elements.sent-2411-002: The size of the run-length encoded BWT (RLBWT) can be interpreted as a measure of repetitiveness in the class of representations called dictionary compression which are essentially representations based on copy and paste operations.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we shed new light on the compressiveness of BWT and the bijective BWT (BBWT).
elements.sent-2411-004: We first extend previous results on the relations of their run-length compressed sizes $r$ and $r_B$.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also show that the so-called ``clustering effect'' of BWT and BBWT can be captured by measures other than empirical entropy or run-length encoding.
elements.sent-2411-006: In particular, we show that BWT and BBWT do not increase the repetitiveness of the string with respect to various measures based on dictionary compression by more than a polylogarithmic factor.
elements.sent-2411-007: Furthermore, we show that there exists an infinite family of strings that are maximally incompressible by any dictionary compression measure, but become very compressible after applying BBWT.
elements.sent-2411-008: An interesting implication of this result is that it is possible to transcend dictionary compression in some cases by simply applying BBWT before applying dictionary compression.
elements.sent-2411-009: The Burrows-Wheeler Transform (BWT)~\cite{bwt94 is a reversible mapping from a string to another string that enables compression and efficient pattern search, and is the theoretical cornerstone for essential tools in the field of bioinformatics~\cite{bowtie09,10.1093/bioinformatics/btp324 .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The compressibility of BWT has been studied in various contexts, but more recently, rather than statistical measures such as empirical entropy which are not helpful in highly repetitive datasets, the size $r$ of the run-length encoded BWT (RLBWT) and its relation to the many other repetitiveness measures related to dictionary compression has become an important topic of study (See~\cite{DBLP:journals/csur/Navarro21 for a comprehensive survey).}
elements.sent-2411-011: Dictionary compression is a family of compressed representations which are essentially based on copy and paste operations.
elements.sent-2411-012: Kempa and Prezza~\cite{DBLP:conf/stoc/KempaP18 proposed the notion of {\em string attractors to view dictionary compression in a uniform way, and showed that the size $\gamma$ of the smallest string attractor lower bounds all other measures of dictionary compression, since they implicitly give a string attractor of the same size as their representations: the size $b$ of the smallest bidirectional macro scheme (BMS)~\cite{DBLP:journals/jacm/StorerS82 , the size $z$ of the LZ77 parsing~\cite{DBLP:journals/tit/ZivL77 , the size $g_{\mathrm{rl }$ of the smallest grammar with run-length rules, the size $g$ of the smallest grammar, $r$, and more.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Another measure $\delta$ based on substring complexity is known to lowerbound $\gamma$~\cite{9961143 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Concerning $r$ in relation to the other measures, Navarro et al.~\cite{DBLP:journals/tit/NavarroOP21 showed that given an RLBWT of size $r$, we can construct a BMS of size $2r$, thus showing $b = O(r)$.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Kempa and Kociumaka~\cite{DBLP:journals/cacm/KempaK22 showed $r = O(z\log^2 n)$, and further $r=O(\delta\log^2 n)$\footnote{More precisely, $r=O(\delta\log \delta\max(1,\log\frac{n {\delta\log \delta ))$}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Also, $z = O(b\log n)$~\cite{DBLP:journals/tit/NavarroOP21 has been shown, and therefore $z = O(r \log n)$ or, $r = \Omega(z/\log n)$.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Recently, the size $r_B$ of the run-length encoded bijective BWT (RLBBWT) has been studied~\cite{DBLP:conf/ictcs/0002CLR23,DBLP:conf/spire/BadkobehBK24 , and was shown, similarly to $r$, that $r_B = O(z\log^2 n)$~\cite{DBLP:conf/spire/BadkobehBK24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Also, the existence of an infinite family of strings such that $r = o(r_B)$, namely, $r = O(1)$ and $r_B = \Omega(\log n)$ was shown~\cite{DBLP:conf/spire/BadkobehBK24 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: The existence of the opposite case, i.e., if there are string families for which $r_B = o(r)$, was left open.
elements.sent-2411-020: The ``clustering effect'' of the BWT in terms of the run length encoding was studied by Mantaci et al.~\cite{DBLP:journals/tcs/MantaciRRSV17 , where they proved that BWT can increase the size of the run-length encoding of the string by a factor of at most $2$.}
elements.sent-2411-021: The maximal clustering effect for run-length encoding can be seen for example with the Fibonacci words: Fibonacci words of length $n$ have a run-length encoding of size $\Theta(n)$, while $r=2$.
elements.sent-2411-022: While this is remarkable when viewed from run-length encoding since a maximally incompressible string (in terms of rle) is transformed into a compressible one, in terms of the smallest bidirectional macro scheme, Fibonacci words and their BWT have the same size $4$, and one might argue that BWT is merely transforming a compressible string into another compressible string.
elements.sent-2411-023: In this paper, we extend previous results on the relation between $r$ and $r_B$.
elements.sent-2411-024: We also investigate the clustering effect of BWT and BBWT in terms of other repetitiveness measures and show that they can be much more powerful than previously perceived, giving an example where BBWT transforms a maximally incompressible string (in terms of dictionary compression) into a compressible one.
elements.sent-2411-025: The contributions of this paper are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-026: We show an infinite family of strings such that $r = \Omega(\log n)$ and $r_B = O(1)$, answering affirmatively an open question about the existence of a family of strings where $r_B = o(r)$ raised by Badkobeh et al.~\cite{DBLP:conf/spire/BadkobehBK24 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: We show a polylogarithmic ($O(\log^4 n)$) upper bound on the multiplicative difference between $r$ and $r_B$.
elements.sent-2411-028: We analyze the clustering effect of BWT and BBWT in terms of other repetitiveness measures, and show that the application of BWT and BBWT can only increase various repetitiveness measures of a string by a polylogarithmic factor.
elements.sent-2411-029: We show that BBWT on the strings whose BBWT images are Fibonacci words exhibit a maximal clustering effect in terms of the repetitiveness measures $\delta$, $\gamma$, $b$, $v$, or $r$, from almost linear ($\Theta(n/\log n)$) to constant.
elements.sent-2411-030: The main combinatorial part of our proof is an elegant characterization of the LF mapping function on Fibonacci strings that uses the Zeckendorf representation of the positions.
elements.sent-2411-031: As a byproduct, this result shows that it is possible in some cases to transcend dictionary compression by simply applying BBWT before applying dictionary compression.
elements.sent-2411-032: Let $\Sigma$ denote a set of symbols called the alphabet.
elements.sent-2411-033: A string is an element of $\Sigma^*$.
elements.sent-2411-034: If $w = xyz$ for any strings $w,x,y,z$, then, $x$, $y$, $z$ are respectively a {\em prefix , {\em substring , and {\em suffix of $w$ and are a {\em proper prefix, substring, or suffix if they are not equal to $w$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The length of string $x$ is denoted by $|x|$.
elements.sent-2411-036: The empty string, i.e., the string of length $0$ is denoted by $\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-037: The symbol at position $i$ will be denoted by $x[i]$, and we will use a $0$-based index, i.e., $x = x[0]\cdots x[|x|-1]$.
elements.sent-2411-038: For integers $i,j\in [0,|x|)$, let $x[i..j] = x[i]\cdots x[j]$ if $i \leq j$ and $x[i..j] = \varepsilon$ if $i > j$.
elements.sent-2411-040: For any string $x$, $x^0 = \varepsilon$, and for any integer $i \geq 1$, $x^i = x^{i-1 x$.}
elements.sent-2411-041: A string $w$ is {\em primitive if it cannot be represented as $w=x^k$ for some string $x$ and integer $k\geq 2$.}
elements.sent-2411-042: For any symbol $c\in\Sigma$ let $|x|_c = |\{ i \mid x[i] = c\}|$, i.e., the number of $c$'s in $x$.
elements.sent-2411-043: For any string $x$, let $\rot(x) = x[1..|x|)x[0]$, denote a left cyclic rotation of $x$ by one symbol.
elements.sent-2411-044: Notice that for any integer $i$, $\rot^i(x)$ naturally corresponds to the cyclic rotation of $x$ by $i$ symbols to the left if $i > 0$, and to the right if $i < 0$.
elements.sent-2411-045: We will use $\rot^*(x)$ to denote the lexicographic smallest rotation of $x$.
elements.sent-2411-046: A {\em conjugacy class is an equivalence class of the equivalence relation defined by $x\equiv y \iff \rot^*(x) = \rot^*(y)$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: A string $w$ is a {\em Lyndon word if it is lexicographically smaller than any of its, proper rotations, i.e., $w < \rot^k(w)$ for any $k\in [1,|w|)$.}
elements.sent-2411-048: From the definition, it is clear that a Lyndon word must be primitive.
elements.sent-2411-049: A rotation of a primitive string $x$ is always primitive and $\rot^*(x)$ is Lyndon and unique, which we will sometimes refer to as the Lyndon rotation of $x$.
elements.sent-2411-050: It is known that Lyndon words cannot have a non-empty proper border (a proper prefix that is also a proper suffix).
elements.sent-2411-051: The Lyndon factorization~\cite{chen58lyndon of a string $w$ is a unique partitioning of $w$ into a sequence of non-increasing Lyndon words, i.e., $w = L^{e_1 _1 \cdots L^{e_{\ell(w) _{\ell(w)}$ where each $L_i~(i\in [1,\ell(w)])$, which we will call {\em Lyndon factors of $w$, is a Lyndon word and $L_{i > L_{i+1}$ for $i \in [1,\ell(w))$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: It is known that any occurrence of a Lyndon word in $w$ must be a substring of a Lyndon factor of $w$.
elements.sent-2411-053: The $\omega$-order $<_\omega$ between primitive strings or same length strings $x,y$ is defined as $x<_\omega y \iff x^\infty < y^\infty$.\footnote{As we will not be comparing non-primitive strings of different lengths in this paper, the definition here is a simplified version of the original.}
elements.sent-2411-054: The $\omega$-order can be different from the standard lexicographic order, but are identical when comparing strings of the same length or when comparing Lyndon words.
elements.sent-2411-055: For a string $x[0..|x|)$, let $\rank_{c (i,x) = |x[0..i)|_c$, i.e., the number of symbols $c$ in $x[0..i)$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Let $\rho(x)$ denote the size of the run-length encoding of $x$, i.e., the maximal number of same symbol runs in $x$.
elements.sent-2411-057: Let $\rho_c(x)$ denote the number of runs of symbol $c$ in the run-length encoding of $x$.
elements.sent-2411-058: A set of positions $\Gamma$ is a string attractor~\cite{DBLP:conf/stoc/KempaP18 of $w$ if any substring of $w$ has an occurrence in $w$ that covers a position in $\Gamma$.}
elements.sent-2411-059: The size of the smallest string attractor of $w$ is denoted by $\gamma(w)$.
elements.sent-2411-060: The measure $\delta$~\cite{9961143 is defined as $\max_{k\in[1,|w|] S_k/k$, where $S_k$ is the number of distinct length-$k$ substrings of $w$.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The Burrows-Wheeler transform (BWT)~\cite{bwt94 $\bwt(w)$ of a string $w$ is defined as the sequence of last (or equivalently, previous, in the cyclic sense) symbols of all rotations of $w$, in lexicographic order of the rotations.}
elements.sent-2411-062: The size of the run-length encoding of $\bwt(w)$ will be denoted by $r(w)$, i.e., $r(w) = \rho(\bwt(w))$.
elements.sent-2411-063: The bijective BWT (BBWT)~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1201-3077 $\bbwt(w)$ of a string $w$ is defined as the sequence of last (or again, previous, in the cyclic sense) symbols of all the rotations of all the Lyndon factors of $w$, in $\omega$-order of the rotations.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Actually, BWT can be understood as a special case of BBWT: $\bwt(w) = \bbwt(\rot^*(w))$ because $\rot^*(w) = L^e$ for some Lyndon word $L$ and integer $e = |w|/|L|$, and the $\omega$-order is equivalent to lexicographic order in this case since all the compared strings are of the same length.
elements.sent-2411-065: The size of the run-length encoding of $\bbwt(w)$ will be denoted by $r_B(w)$, i.e., $r_B(w) = \rho(\bbwt(w))$.
elements.sent-2411-066: The inverse transform of $\bwt$ and $\bbwt$ on a string $x$ can be defined by the {\em LF mapping , which is a function $\Psi_x(i) = j$ over positions of $x$ where $c = x[i] = s[j]$, $\rank_{c (i,x) = \rank_{c (j,s)$, and $s$ is the string obtained by sorting the multiset of symbols of $x$ in increasing order.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: A cycle $(i, \Psi^1(i), \cdots, \Psi^{k-1 (i))$ where $k$ is the smallest positive integer such that $\Psi^{k (i) = i$, corresponds to a (cyclic) string $x[\Psi^{k-1 (i)]\cdots x[\Psi^1(i)] x[i]$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: This string is always primitive, and by concatenating, in non-increasing order, all the Lyndon rotations of the strings corresponding to all cycles, it can be shown that $\bbwt^{-1 (x)$ is obtained.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Note that when $x$ is a $\bwt$ image of a primitive string, $\Psi_x$ consists of only a single cycle and that although the Lyndon rotation will give the string $w$ for which $\bbwt(w) = x$, we have $\bwt(w') = x$ for any rotation $w'$ of $w$.
elements.sent-2411-070: A Bidirectional Macro Scheme (BMS)~\cite{DBLP:journals/jacm/StorerS82 of a string $w$ is a partitioning of $w$ into phrases, where each phrase is either a single symbol, or is a substring that has an occurrence elsewhere in $w$ which we call the source, or the reference of the phrase.}
elements.sent-2411-071: The references of the phrases must be such that the induced reference for each position in the phrases are acyclic, i.e., the referencing on the position forms a forest where the roots are the positions corresponding to single symbol phrases.
elements.sent-2411-072: The size of the smallest bidirectional macro scheme for $w$ is denoted by $b(w)$.
elements.sent-2411-073: The LZ77 factorization~\cite{DBLP:journals/tit/ZivL77 of a string $w$ is a BMS of $w$ where all references are left-referencing, i.e., they must point to a smaller position, and the phrases are determined greedily from left-to-right.}
elements.sent-2411-074: It is known that LZ77 is the smallest among left-referencing BMS.
elements.sent-2411-075: The size of LZ77 of $w$ is denoted by $z(w)$.
elements.sent-2411-076: The lex-parse~\cite{DBLP:journals/tit/NavarroOP21 of a string $w$ is a BMS of $w$ where all references point to a rotation of smaller lexicographic (or equivalently $\omega$-order) rotation, and the phrases are determined greedily from left-to-right.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Similarly, it is known that lex-parse is the smallest among BMS with such constraint.
elements.sent-2411-078: The size of lex-parse of $w$ is denoted by $v(w)$.
elements.sent-2411-079: Since we will later use Fibonacci words or their slight modifications to show some of our results, we introduce them here.
elements.sent-2411-080: The Fibonacci words are defined recursively as: $F_0 = \mathtt{b}$, $F_1 = \mathtt{a}$, and for any integer $i \geq 2$, $F_i = F_{i-1 F_{i-2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Fibonacci words can also be defined via a morphism $\phi$ defined as $\phi(\mathtt{a ) = \mathtt{ab}$, and $\phi(\mathtt{b ) = \mathtt{a}$, and $F_i = \phi^i(\mathtt{b )$ for all $i \geq 0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The lengths of the Fibonacci words correspond to the Fibonacci sequence, i.e., $f_0 = f_1 = 1$, and $f_i = f_{i-1 +f_{i-2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-083: For technical reasons, we define $f_i = 0$ for $i < 0$.

elements.sent-2411-166: We first recall the arguments of~\cite{DBLP:journals/tcs/MantaciRRSV17 for BWT.}
elements.sent-2411-167: Consider a range $[i..j]$ of $x = \bwt(w)$ that correspond to rotations of $w$ that start with a symbol $c\in\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-168: Then, $x[i..j]$ will consist of $|w|_c - \rho_c(w)$ occurrences of $c$'s that correspond to those preceding a $c$ in the same run of $c$'s in $w$, and $\rho_c(w)$ occurrence of symbols not equal to $c$ that precede a maximal run of $c$'s in $w$.
elements.sent-2411-169: Thus, $\rho(x[i..j])$ is maximized when the $\rho_c(w)$ non-$c$ symbols in $x[i..j]$ are not adjacent to each other.
elements.sent-2411-170: Mantaci et al. argued that this implies $\rho(x[i..j]) \leq 2\rho_c(w)$ summing up to $2\rho(w)$ for all $c$.
elements.sent-2411-171: However, they did not give a reason to why $\rho(x[i..j])$ could not be $2\rho_c(w)+1$ (summing up to $\rho(w)+\sigma$), which is possibly the case when $x[i..j]$ starts and ends with the symbol $c$.
elements.sent-2411-172: We show that in such a case, an LF mapping with a single cycle cannot be defined, and ultimately, $\rho(x(w)[i..j]) \leq 2\rho_c(w)$ holds.
elements.sent-2411-173: Let $w[i'..j']$ be a maximal run of symbol $c$ in $w$ and let $k$ be such that $x[k]$ corresponds to $w[j']$, i.e., $k$ is the $\omega$-order rank of $\rot^{j'+1 (w)$.}

elements.sent-2411-180: For $x = \bbwt(w)$, similarly consider the range $[i..j]$ of $\bbwt(w)$ such that the corresponding rotations of the Lyndon factors of $w$ start with the symbol $c\in\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-181: Since it is known that Lyndon factors of $w$ (except for single symbol factors) must start and end at run-boundaries of $w$~\cite{DBLP:conf/isaac/FujishigeNIBT17 , a maximal run of $c$'s in $w$ is either a substring of a Lyndon factor of $w$, or a maximal run of a single symbol Lyndon factors.}
elements.sent-2411-182: If it is a substring of a Lyndon factor but not a prefix, then there is one non-$c$ symbol per such run that precedes the run and occurs in $x[i..j]$.
elements.sent-2411-183: If it is a prefix of a Lyndon factor longer than $1$, then the previous symbol (or the last symbol of the Lyndon factor) is non-$c$ due to the Lyndon factor not having a proper border.
elements.sent-2411-184: Otherwise, the maximal run is a maximal run of single symbol Lyndon factors $c$, in which case the previous symbol for these occurring in $x[i..j]$ will also be $c$.
elements.sent-2411-185: Thus, the arguments in the previous paragraph for BWT hold for BBWT as well, except for the last part: it can be that $x[i]=x[j]=c$ and $\Psi(k')=k'$ for some $k'\in[i,j]$.

elements.subsection-2411-006: In terms of other repetitiveness measures
elements.sent-2411-188: We consider measuring the clustering effect of $\bwt$ and $\bbwt$ other than~$\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-189: Here, we show that $\bwt$ or $\bbwt$ can only increase the repetitiveness of the string by a polylogarithmic factor.
elements.sent-2411-190: For repetitiveness measure $\mu \in \{\delta,\gamma,b,v,z, g_{\mathrm{rl }\}$ and any string $w$ of length $n$, it holds that $\mu(\bwt(w)) = O(\mu(w)\log^2 n)$.}
elements.sent-2411-191: Moreover, $\mu(\bbwt(w)) = O(\mu(w)\log^2 n)$ for $\mu \in \{z, g_{\mathrm{rl }\}$, and $\mu(\bbwt(w)) = O(\mu(w)\log^6 n)$ for $\mu \in \{\delta,\gamma,b,v\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-192: The proof for $\bwt$ follows from a simple observation that for any string $w$, $\mu(w) = O(\rho(w))$ implying $\mu(\bwt(w)) = O(\rho(\bwt(w))$.
elements.sent-2411-193: Since $r(w) = \rho(\bwt(w)) = O(\delta(w)\log^2n)$, we have $\mu(\bwt(w)) = O(\delta(w)\log^2 n) = O(\mu(w)\log^2 n)$, where the last relation follows from the fact that $\delta$ lower bounds all the repetitiveness measures considered.
elements.sent-2411-194: For $\bbwt$, $\mu(\bbwt(w)) = O(\rho(\bbwt(w)))$ holds, but only $r_B(w) = \rho(\bbwt(w)) = O(z(w)\log^2 n)$ has been proved, from which $r_B(w) = O(g_{\mathrm{rl (w)\log^2 n)$ and the statement follows for $\mu\in\{z,g_{\mathrm{rl }\}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-195: For the other measures, \Cref{corollary:RvsRB gives us $r_B(w) = O(r(w)\log^4 n) = O(\delta(w)\log^6 n)$ from which the statement follows.}
elements.subsection-2411-007: A family of significantly ``clustered'' strings by BBWT

elements.sent-2411-197: Namely, we consider the family of strings whose BBWT image are Fibonacci words.
elements.sent-2411-198: Then, for $\mu\in\{\delta,\gamma,b,v,r\}$, $\mu(\bbwt(w)) = O(\frac{\log n {n}\mu(w))$ and for $\mu\in\{z, g_{\mathrm{rl ,g \}$, $\mu(\bbwt(w)) = O(\frac{\log^2 n {n}\mu(w))$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-199: The Zeckendorf representation~\cite{1570009749187075840 of a non-negative integer is a unique (sub-)set of distinct non-consecutive Fibonacci numbers $\{ f_k \mid k \geq 1 \}$ that sum up to the integer.}
elements.sent-2411-200: We represent a non-negative integer $i$ as a bit string $Z(i)$, where $i = ||Z(i)|| = \sum_{j\geq 0 Z(i)[j]\cdot f_{j+1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-201: We use $Z_k(i)$ to denote the length-$k$ prefix $Z(i)[0..k-1]$ of $Z(i)$.
elements.sent-2411-202: Note that for any $i \in [0,f_k)$, it suffices that a subset of $\{ f_1,\dots,f_{k-1}\}$ is used, and thus $Z(i)$ requires only up to $(k-1)$ bits, i.e., $1$'s will only occur in $Z(i)[0..k-2]$, and the rest $Z(i)[k-1..]$ will be $0$.
elements.sent-2411-203: We observe that length $i\geq 0$ prefix of a Fibonacci word can be uniquely factorized into a sequence of distinct non-consecutive Fibonacci words in order of decreasing length, where the length of each Fibonacci word corresponds to an element in the Zeckendorf representation of~$i$.

elements.sent-2411-205: Consider a greedy factorization of $F_\infty[0..i)$ that takes the longest prefix of the remaining string that is a Fibonacci word.
elements.sent-2411-206: Let $k$ be the smallest integer such that $i \leq f_k$.
elements.sent-2411-207: If $i = f_k$ then we simply take $F_k$ as the last element.

elements.sent-2411-210: The remaining string is a proper prefix of $F_{k-2}$ and thus $F_{k-2}$ will not be chosen next, implying that consecutive Fibonacci words are not chosen.
elements.sent-2411-211: Thus, the lengths of the sequence of strings will be a set of non-consecutive set of distinct Fibonacci numbers that sum up to $i$.
elements.sent-2411-212: The lemma follows from the uniqueness of the Zeckendorf representation of~$i$.
elements.sent-2411-213: The following relation about the least significant bit in the Zeckendorf representation and the $i$th symbol in the (infinite) Fibonacci word is known.
elements.sent-2411-214: Now, we make observations on the LF-mapping for Fibonacci words.
elements.sent-2411-215: The LF-mapping function for the Fibonacci word $F_k$ is, for any $0 \leq i < f_k$, \begin{equation}\Psi_{F_k}(i) =  \rank_a(i,F_k) if F_k[i] = a, f_{k-1} + \rank_b(i,F_k) if F_k[i] = b. \end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\Psi_{F_k}(i) =  \rank_a(i,F_k) if F_k[i] = a, f_{k-1} + \rank_b(i,F_k) if F_k[i] = b. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-216: Straightforward from the definition of $\Psi_{F_k}$ and \Cref{observation:NumberOfAorB .}
elements.sent-2411-217: The next lemma shows that the LF mappings on $F_k$ can be interpreted as rotation operations on the Zeckendorf representation of the position to be mapped.
elements.sent-2411-218: For any $i \in [0,f_k)$, \begin{equation}Z_k(\Psi_{F_k}(i)) =  \rot(Z_k(i)) if $F_k[i] = a$, \rot^2(Z_k(i)) if $F_k[i] = b$. \end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-220: Since $i, j\in [0,f_{k )$, the most significant bits of $Z_k(i)$ and $Z_k(j)$, corresponding to $f_{k}$, are always zero, i.e., $Z_k(i)[k-1]= Z_k(j)[k-1] = 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-223: Therefore, we have \begin{equation}\Psi_{F_k}(i) =\rank_a(i,F_k) \text{by \Cref{lemma:PsiOfFibonacci}} =\left|\prod_{j=k-2}^0F_{j+1}^{Z(i)[j]}\right|_a \text{by \Cref{lemma:fibonacciStringWithNumeration}} =\sum_{j=0}^{k-2}Z_k(i)[j]\cdot f_j =\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}Z_k(i)[j]\cdot f_j =Z_k(i)[0]\cdot f_k + \sum_{j=0}^{k-2}Z_k(i)[j+1]\cdot f_{j+1} =\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}Z_k(i)[(j+1)\bmod k]\cdot f_{j+1} =||\rot(Z_k(i))||\end{equation} On the other hand, again from \Cref{lemma:firstBitOfZ , $F_k[i] = \mathtt{b}$ implies that $Z_k(i)[0] = 1$ and since the Zeckendorf representation does not use consecutive Fibonacci numbers, $Z_k(i)[1]=0$.}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\Psi_{F_k}(i) =\rank_a(i,F_k) \text{by \Cref{lemma:PsiOfFibonacci}} =\left|\prod_{j=k-2}^0F_{j+1}^{Z(i)[j]}\right|_a \text{by \Cref{lemma:fibonacciStringWithNumeration}} =\sum_{j=0}^{k-2}Z_k(i)[j]\cdot f_j =\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}Z_k(i)[j]\cdot f_j =Z_k(i)[0]\cdot f_k + \sum_{j=0}^{k-2}Z_k(i)[j+1]\cdot f_{j+1} =\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}Z_k(i)[(j+1)\bmod k]\cdot f_{j+1} =||\rot(Z_k(i))||\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-225: Therefore, we have \begin{equation}\rank_b(i,F_k) =\left|\prod_{j=k-2}^0F_{j+1}^{Z(i)[j]}\right|_b \text{by \Cref{lemma:fibonacciStringWithNumeration}} =\sum_{j=0}^{k-2}Z_k(i)[j]\cdot f_{j-1}=\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}Z_k(i)[j]\cdot f_{j-1} =Z_k(i)[0]\cdot f_{-1} + Z_k(i)[1]\cdot f_{0} + \sum_{j=0}^{k-3}Z_k(i)[j+2]\cdot f_{j+1} = \sum_{j=0}^{k-3}Z_k(i)[j+2]\cdot f_{j+1}\end{equation} Furthermore, \begin{equation}\Psi_{F_k}(i) =\rank_b(i,F_k) + f_{k-1} \text{by~\Cref{lemma:PsiOfFibonacci}} = \sum_{j=0}^{k-3}Z_k(i)[j+2]\cdot f_{j+1} + f_{k-1} = \sum_{j=0}^{k-3}Z_k(i)[j+2]\cdot f_{j+1} + Z_k(i)[0]\cdot f_{k-1} + Z_k(i)[1]\cdot f_{k} = \sum_{j=0}^{k-1}Z_k(i)[(j+2)\bmod k]\cdot f_{j+1} = ||\rot^2(Z_k(i))||.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}\rank_b(i,F_k) =\left|\prod_{j=k-2}^0F_{j+1}^{Z(i)[j]}\right|_b \text{by \Cref{lemma:fibonacciStringWithNumeration}} =\sum_{j=0}^{k-2}Z_k(i)[j]\cdot f_{j-1}=\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}Z_k(i)[j]\cdot f_{j-1} =Z_k(i)[0]\cdot f_{-1} + Z_k(i)[1]\cdot f_{0} + \sum_{j=0}^{k-3}Z_k(i)[j+2]\cdot f_{j+1} = \sum_{j=0}^{k-3}Z_k(i)[j+2]\cdot f_{j+1}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}\Psi_{F_k}(i) =\rank_b(i,F_k) + f_{k-1} \text{by~\Cref{lemma:PsiOfFibonacci}} = \sum_{j=0}^{k-3}Z_k(i)[j+2]\cdot f_{j+1} + f_{k-1} = \sum_{j=0}^{k-3}Z_k(i)[j+2]\cdot f_{j+1} + Z_k(i)[0]\cdot f_{k-1} + Z_k(i)[1]\cdot f_{k} = \sum_{j=0}^{k-1}Z_k(i)[(j+2)\bmod k]\cdot f_{j+1} = ||\rot^2(Z_k(i))||.\end{equation}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{fig_Fib-LFmap.pdf} \caption{ Example of Lemma~\ref{lemma:PsiOfFibIsRotation} for $F_7$. The $\mathtt{a}$ at position $18$ is the $12$th $\mathtt{a}$ in $F_7[0..18]$, and thus the LF mapping should point to position $11$, whose Zeckendorf representation $Z_7(11)$ is a 1-bit left rotation of $Z_7(18)$. The $\mathtt{b}$ at position $17$ is the $7$th $\mathtt{b}$ in $F_7[0..17]$, and since there are $13$ $\mathtt{a}$'s in $F_7$, the LF mapping should point to position $13+7-1=19$, whose Zeckendorf representation $Z_7(19)$ is a 2-bit left rotation of $Z_7(17)$. } \label{fig:Fib-LFmap} \end{center}

elements.sent-2411-230: Therefore, the number of cycles that $\Psi_{F_k}$ produces is equivalent to the number of conjugacy classes among bit strings corresponding to the Zeckendorf representations of the set of positions $[0,F_k)$.
elements.sent-2411-231: Since the Zeckendorf representations do not use adjacent Fibonacci numbers, this is equivalent to the number $C(k)$ of binary necklaces of length $k$ that do not contain ``11''.
elements.sent-2411-232: This corresponds to entry A000358 in the on-line encyclopedia of integer sequences, which is known~\cite{bona2015handbook to be: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:ck} C(k) = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{d|k}\varphi(k/d)(f_{d-2}+f_{d}),\end{equation} where $d|k$ represents that $d$ is a divisor of $k$, and $\varphi$ is Euler's totient function which returns, for integer $n$, the number of positive integers less than $n$ that are relatively prime to $n$ (i.e., having $\gcd$ of $1$).}
